title: Mince pies
subtitle: Sweet shortcrust cases filled with spiced mincemeat
excerpt: Twelve mince pies in a standard 12-hole bun tin: a buttery sweet shortcrust case filled with shop-bought or homemade mincemeat and baked until pale gold. The pastry is enriched with icing sugar and egg yolk, which makes it more delicate and crumbly than plain shortcrust.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the mince pie chapter documents a rich sweet shortcrust case filled with spiced fruit mincemeat, baked and dusted with icing sugar. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the pastry section documents sweet shortcrust enriched with icing sugar and egg yolk as the correct case for fruit tarts, producing a more tender crumb than plain shortcrust. Mince pies using spiced dried fruit and suet in a sweet pastry case are documented continuously in British domestic cookery from the Victorian period onward.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked assembled pies in the tin, then transfer to a bag. Bake from frozen, adding 5 minutes to the baking time.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily; roll and cut pastry in two batches.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pastry dough keeps refrigerated for 2 days. The assembled, unbaked pies keep refrigerated overnight.

recipeTools.notes: A 7.5 cm round cutter for bases and a 6.5 cm cutter (or star) for lids. A glass or cup works if you have no cutter.

glossaryTerms.definition: Shortcrust pastry enriched with icing sugar and egg yolk instead of water. The icing sugar dissolves without graininess and the egg yolk adds richness, producing a pastry that is more delicate and crumbly than plain shortcrust — and more prone to cracking when rolled, which is why a chill is essential before working it. Used for sweet tart cases and mince pies where a tender bite is the goal.

content.text: The mince pie is one of the few baked goods in British cooking that truly improves with rest. A tray made on Christmas Eve will be better the next morning, when the pastry has softened slightly against the mincemeat and the flavour has settled. The pastry here is
: enriched with icing sugar and egg yolk, more delicate and crumbly than plain shortcrust, which holds together long enough to eat but shatters at the first bite.

items.prepNote: to dust warm pies before serving

content.text: Combine the {{plain-flour}}, {{icing-sugar}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a bowl. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} cold butter and rub in with your fingertips until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs with a few pea-sized pieces remaining. Add the {{egg-yolks}} egg yolks and 1–2 teaspoons of cold water. Mix with a round-bladed knife until the dough just comes together. Press into a disc, wrap, and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Grease a 12-hole bun tin. On a lightly floured surface, roll the pastry to 3 mm thick. Cut 12 circles for the bases using a 7.5 cm round cutter and press them into the tin cups, easing them in without stretching. Cut 12 lids using a 6.5 cm round cutter, or use a star shape. Spoon a heaped teaspoon of {{mincemeat}} into each case — do not overfill or the mincemeat will bubble over the sides and glue the pies in.

Brush the pastry edges with {{eggs}} egg wash. Press the lids on and pinch gently to seal. Brush the tops with egg wash. Bake for 16–18 minutes, until the pastry is pale golden — sweet shortcrust burns more quickly than plain, so watch the colour. Leave in the tin for 5 minutes before easing out with a palette knife. Dust with {{icing-sugar}} icing sugar while still warm.
